---
title: "Game of Thrones - Viewership Report"
author: "Tsoi Kwan Ma - 476914"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Contents"
    number-sections: false
    theme: flatly
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
knitr::opts_knit$set(root.dir = "/Users/matsoikwan/Library/Mobile Documents/com~apple~CloudDocs/University of Warsaw/Reproducible Research/RRcourse2026/Data")

library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)
library(scales)
```

```{r}
#| label: load-data
#| include: false

# Read all 8 season files in a loop and combine into one data frame
n_seasons <- 8

episodes <- map_dfr(1:n_seasons, function(s) {
  read_csv(
    paste0("season_", s, ".csv"),
    show_col_types = FALSE
  ) %>%
    mutate(season = s)
})

# Keep only the columns we need and clean up
episodes <- episodes %>%
  select(season, no_season, title, premiere_date, viewers) %>%
  rename(ep_in_season = no_season) %>%
  mutate(
    ep_overall = row_number() # Running episode number across all seasons
  )

# Season-level summary
season_avgs <- episodes %>%
  group_by(season) %>%
  summarise(
    n_episodes = n(),
    avg_viewers = round(mean(viewers), 2),
    max_viewers = max(viewers),
    min_viewers = min(viewers)
  )

# Season-to-season change in average viewership
season_avgs <- season_avgs %>%
  mutate(change = round(avg_viewers - lag(avg_viewers), 2))
```

---

## About the Show

*Game of Thrones* is an American fantasy drama television series created by *David Benioff* and *D. B. Weiss* for *HBO*. It is the first adaptation of the *A Song of Ice and Fire* franchise, a series of high fantasy novels by *George R. R. Martin*, the first of which is *A Game of Thrones*. The show premiered on HBO in the *United States* on April 17, 2011, and concluded on May 19, 2019, with 73 episodes broadcast over 8 seasons.

*Game of Thrones* attracted a record viewership on *HBO* and has a broad, active, and international fan base. Many critics and publications have named the show one of the greatest television series of all time. Critics have praised the series for its acting, complex characters, story, scope, and production values, although its frequent use of nudity and violence (including sexual violence) generated controversy. The final season received significant criticism for its reduced length and creative decisions, with many considering it a disappointing conclusion. The series received 59 *Primetime Emmy Awards*, the most by a drama series, including *Outstanding Drama Series* in 2015, 2016, 2018 and 2019. Its other awards and nominations include three *Hugo Awards for Best Dramatic Presentation*, a *Peabody Award*, and five nominations for the *Golden Globe Award for Best Television Series – Drama*.


![Game of Thrones title card. All contents and the picture were retrieved from Wikipedia.](Game_of_Thrones_title_card.png){width=60% fig-align="center"}

---

## Viewership Statistics

### Season Summary Table

The table below summarizes the average, peak, and lowest US viewership (in millions) for each season, along with the change relative to the previous season.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-season-summary

season_avgs %>%
  mutate(
    change = ifelse(is.na(change), "—", paste0(ifelse(change >= 0, "+", ""), change))
  ) %>%
  kable(
    col.names = c("Season", "Episodes", "Avg. Viewers", "Peak", "Lowest", "Change vs. Prev."),
    align = "cccccc",
    caption = "US viewership by season (in millions of viewers)"
  )
```

---

## Viewership Over Time

### Episode-by-episode viewership

The chart below shows the viewership (in millions) for every episode across all 8 seasons. Each season is shown in a distinct color.

```{r}
#| label: fig-viewership-over-time
#| fig-cap: "Episode-by-episode US viewership of *Game of Thrones* (millions)"
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 5

ggplot(episodes, aes(x = ep_overall, y = viewers, colour = factor(season))) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(size = 1.8) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Dark2", name = "Season") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1, max(episodes$ep_overall), by = 5)) +
  labs(
    x = "Episode (overall)",
    y = "Viewers (millions)",
    title = "Game of Thrones - Episode Viewership"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

### Season-average viewership

```{r}
#| label: fig-season-avg
#| fig-cap: "Average US viewership per season of *Game of Thrones* (millions)"
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 4.5

ggplot(season_avgs, aes(x = season, y = avg_viewers)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#2c7bb6", width = 0.65) +
  geom_text(aes(label = avg_viewers), vjust = -0.5, size = 3.8) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:n_seasons) +
  labs(
    x = "Season",
    y = "Average Viewers (millions)",
    title = "Game of Thrones - Average Viewership by Season"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)
```

---

## Season-to-Season Changes

### Change in average viewership relative to the previous season

The chart below highlights how much average viewership grew or declined between consecutive seasons.

```{r}
#| label: fig-season-change
#| fig-cap: "Season-over-season change in average viewership (millions)"
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 4.5

season_avgs %>%
  filter(!is.na(change)) %>%
  mutate(direction = ifelse(change >= 0, "Increase", "Decrease")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = season, y = change, fill = direction)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.65) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, colour = "black", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(ifelse(change >= 0, "+", ""), change)),
            vjust = ifelse(season_avgs$change[-1] >= 0, -0.5, 1.4),
            size = 3.8) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 2:n_seasons) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Increase" = "#1a9641", "Decrease" = "#d7191c"),
                    name = NULL) +
  labs(
    x = "Season",
    y = "Change in Avg. Viewers (millions)",
    title = "Game of Thrones - Season-over-Season Viewership Change"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

---

## Observations

```{r}
#| label: inline-numbers
#| include: false

# Pull specific numbers for inline use
avg_s1    <- season_avgs$avg_viewers[1]
avg_s8    <- season_avgs$avg_viewers[8]
peak_ep   <- episodes %>% slice_max(viewers, n = 1)
total_growth <- round(avg_s8 - avg_s1, 2)

# Biggest single-season jump
biggest_jump <- season_avgs %>% filter(!is.na(change)) %>% slice_max(change, n = 1)
biggest_drop <- season_avgs %>% filter(!is.na(change)) %>% slice_min(change, n = 1)
```

*Game of Thrones* showed remarkable viewership growth throughout most of its run. The show launched with an average of **`r avg_s1` million viewers** in Season 1 and grew steadily, afterwards reaching a peak season average of **`r season_avgs$avg_viewers[8]` million viewers** in Season 8.

The single largest season-over-season jump occurred between Seasons `r biggest_jump$season - 1` and `r biggest_jump$season` where average viewership increased by **`r biggest_jump$change` million viewers**. The most-watched episode of the entire series attracted **`r peak_ep$viewers` million viewers** (*`r peak_ep$title`*, Season `r peak_ep$season`), which is the recorded highest single-episode viewership in the show's history. It reflects the massive audience drawn in by the series finale.

Overall, from Season 1 to Season 8, the average viewership grew by **`r total_growth` million viewers** and it made *Game of Thrones* be one of the most successful television series in terms of audience growth and retention.
